grants.name: Gambling Community Benefit Fund (GCBF)
grants.provider: Queensland Government — Dept of Justice and Attorney-General
grants.nextRound: Multiple rounds per year — check portal for current dates

eligibility.whoCanApply: Not-for-profit community organisations in Queensland, including incorporated sport and recreation clubs
requirements: Incorporated association or similar legal structure
eligibility.croquetNotes: Individual QLD croquet clubs can and have successfully applied. Southport Croquet Club won $35,000 for a veranda. One of the best-fit grants for croquet clubs.

whatItDoesNotFund: Check current round guidelines for specific exclusions
tips: Start planning well before the application deadline — Southport's application took months of preparation.
Gather multiple quotes for any construction or purchase.
Show the project benefits more than just croquet players — community impact is key.
Find a dedicated grant writer in your club — one person who enjoys research and paperwork makes a big difference.

lessons: Start early and give yourself a long lead time
Gather quotes and document community impact thoroughly
Find one person good at applications and let them own it
Build council relationships alongside grant applications

grants.name: Active Industry Base Fund (AIBF)
grants.provider: Queensland Government — Dept of Sport, Racing and Olympic and Paralympic Games
grants.nextRound: Current cycle ends 30 June 2026. Next round not yet announced — contact department to register interest.

eligibility.whoCanApply: State-level not-for-profit sporting organisations in Queensland
requirements: Activities statewide with affiliates in 3+ sport regions
No more than 1 outstanding annual report with OFT
eligibility.croquetNotes: This is for CAQ (the state body), NOT individual clubs. CAQ meets the geographic footprint requirement with 41 clubs across Queensland.

whatItDoesNotFund: Physical infrastructure (separate programs like Games On! cover this)
tips: MANDATORY: Contact sportrecpartnership@dtis.qld.gov.au before any closing date to request application templates.
Email the department NOW to introduce CAQ and register for next round notifications.
Gender Equity in Sports Governance targets must be met by July 2029 — plan accordingly.
Cost your planned activities specifically and justify the amount you request.

grants.name: Games On! Grassroots Infrastructure Program
grants.provider: Queensland Government — Dept of Sport, Racing and Olympic and Paralympic Games
grants.nextRound: Announced February 2026 — check department portal for current status

eligibility.whoCanApply: Grassroots sport and recreation organisations in Queensland
requirements: Check program guidelines for specific criteria
eligibility.croquetNotes: Likely eligible — croquet is a grassroots sport. Clubs needing court upgrades, lighting, or clubhouse improvements should investigate.

whatItDoesNotFund: Administrative or operational costs (AIBF covers that)
tips: This is specifically for physical infrastructure, not administration.
$30 million is a significant pool — worth pursuing.
Check eligibility guidelines on the department portal as soon as possible.

grants.name: Local Council Community Grants
grants.nextRound: Varies by council — most run 1–2 rounds per year

eligibility.whoCanApply: Community organisations within the council's local government area
requirements: Must operate within the council area
Project must benefit the local community
eligibility.croquetNotes: Southport Croquet Club funded court lights through their local council. Each club should check their own council's website.

tips: Build a relationship with your council BEFORE you need funding.
Present your club as a community asset, not just a sport club.
Council funding is about timing, relationships, and being ready when opportunities arise.
Check your specific council's website for programs and deadlines.

lessons: Built council relationship over several years
Was already on the radar as a well-run club
Council funding works differently from grants — it's about relationships

grants.name: Australian Sports Foundation (Tax-Deductible Donations)
grants.nextRound: Always open — register a project at any time

requirements: Register a specific project with ASF
eligibility.croquetNotes: Clubs can register projects to receive tax-deductible donations. Great for large capital projects — donors get a tax benefit, so they're more likely to give.

whatItFunds: Any sport-related project the club registers
Capital works, equipment, programs and development
tips: Tax-deductibility is a big incentive — donors get a tax benefit.
Good for combining grant funding with community fundraising.
Promote your ASF project page through newsletters, social media, and events.

grants.name: QLD Government Grants Finder
grants.nextRound: Always available — new grants added as they open

eligibility.whoCanApply: Varies by individual grant listed on the portal
eligibility.croquetNotes: Search portal — search regularly for sport, recreation, and community grants. Filter by eligibility for incorporated associations.

whatItFunds: Varies — covers all QLD Government grant programs
tips: Search regularly — new grants appear as they open.
Use terms like 'sport', 'recreation', 'community', 'not-for-profit'.
Subscribe to the QLD Sport & Rec 'State of Play' newsletter for alerts.

grants.name: Sport and Recreation Grant Registration Portal
grants.nextRound: Register to receive notifications when programs open

eligibility.whoCanApply: Sport and recreation organisations in Queensland
eligibility.croquetNotes: Register here to be notified of sport-specific funding as it opens. Gateway to AIBF and other sport programs.

tips: Register to receive notifications when new sport and recreation grants open.
This is the gateway to AIBF applications and other sport-specific programs.

grants.name: Corporate Community Grant Programs
grants.nextRound: Varies by company — check individual program websites

requirements: Typically must be a not-for-profit or community group
Must align with the company's community investment priorities
eligibility.croquetNotes: Frame croquet around themes companies care about: active ageing, social inclusion, community wellbeing, mental health through sport.

tips: Search for programs from major companies in your area.
Frame your club around active ageing, social inclusion, and community wellbeing.
Bendigo Bank, Woolworths, and Bunnings all have community programs.

grants.name: Community Foundations and Philanthropic Trusts

eligibility.whoCanApply: Community groups and not-for-profit organisations, typically within a specific geographic area
requirements: Operate within the foundation's geographic area
eligibility.croquetNotes: Often less competitive than government grants. Frame your club as a community wellbeing and social inclusion asset.

whatItFunds: Programs supporting community wellbeing and inclusion
tips: Search for community foundations in your local area.
Less competitive than government grants — worth trying.
Frame croquet as community wellbeing and social inclusion.
Network with other local community groups to learn about opportunities.

resources.name: Be Grant Ready (QLD Gov Guide)
resources.description: How to prepare your club for grant applications — incorporation, ABN, financials, strategic plan.

resources.name: Grant Writing Guide (QLD Gov)
resources.description: How to write strong grant applications — answering questions, aligning with objectives, budgeting.

resources.name: Making Money (QLD Gov Guide)
resources.description: Fundraising ideas and tips for sport clubs beyond grants.

resources.name: Sponsorship Guide (QLD Gov)
resources.description: Creating effective sponsorship partnerships for your club.

resources.name: Sport and Recreation Partnerships Office
resources.description: Mandatory contact point for AIBF applications. Contact to register interest and request application templates.
